coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effects of MoFe bimetallic carbon dots for enhanced photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Jianuan
Li, Kang
Shan, Yuchao
Cai, Wei
Qu, Hongxia
coredata.dc:description: The fixation of nitrogen to ammonia by photocatalysis displays great prospects. To this end, designing efficient catalysts is vital. As a type of graphitic carbon nitride, g-C3N5 is rich in N atoms, which endows it with a superior energy band structure. To compensate for the lack of active sites and the fast recombination of photogenerated carriers, we constructed MoFe bimetallic carbon dots loaded g-C3N5, with an NH3 yield of 812.9 μmol h−1 gcat −1. The introduction of metal carbon dots facilitates charge transfer. Through further study, it was found that Fe sites are mainly responsible for activating nitrogen, while Mo sites tend to split water to produce protons. Bimetallic carbon dots showed a synergistic effect on the enhancement of nitrogen fixation performance. This work offers some insights for the construction of efficient nitrogen fixation catalysts with novel carbon dots types of active centers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
Bimetallic carbon dots
N2 activation
Proton layer
Synergistic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance and the kinetic profile
Photoelectric property, band structure and N2 adsorption
The role of Mo/Fe sites in carbon dots and reaction mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The NH3 yields of different samples under N2 (35 °C). (b) multicycle N2 fixation, (c) time dependent NH3 yield profile under different conditions, (d) the NH3 yields at different temperature, (e) TOFs for photocatalytic N2 fixation under different temperature, (f) Arrhenius plot over MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100.
(a, b) TEM images, (c) HRTEM images and (d–h) HAADF‐STEM image with corresponding elemental mappings of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra, (b) Tauc plots, (c) Mott-Schottky curves and (d) Electronic energy-level diagram of g-C3N4, g-C3N5 and MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100.
(a) N2-TPD profiles of g-C3N5 and M-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (M=Mo, Fe, MoFe). (b, c) High-resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d and Fe 2p for MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100.
(a) High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s for MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100. (b) Schematic diagram of the roles of Mo and Fe sites.
In-situ DRFTIR spectra recorded during the photocatalytic N2 fixation over (a) g-C3N5, (b) Fe-CDs@g-C3N5–100, (c) Mo-CDs@g-C3N5–100 and (d) MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100.
Schematic diagram of the photocatalytic N2 fixation process over MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effects of MoFe bimetallic carbon dots for enhanced photocatalytic nitrogen fixation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemical Engineering and Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Norinco International Cooperation Ltd., Beijing 100071, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNorinco International Cooperation Ltd., Beijing 100071, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fixation of nitrogen to ammonia by photocatalysis displays great prospects. To this end, designing efficient catalysts is vital. As a type of graphitic carbon nitride, g-C3N5 is rich in N atoms, which endows it with a superior energy band structure. To compensate for the lack of active sites and the fast recombination of photogenerated carriers, we constructed MoFe bimetallic carbon dots loaded g-C3N5, with an NH3 yield of 812.9 μmol h−1 gcat −1. The introduction of metal carbon dots facilitates charge transfer. Through further study, it was found that Fe sites are mainly responsible for activating nitrogen, while Mo sites tend to split water to produce protons. Bimetallic carbon dots showed a synergistic effect on the enhancement of nitrogen fixation performance. This work offers some insights for the construction of efficient nitrogen fixation catalysts with novel carbon dots types of active centers.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The artificial fixation of nitrogen into ammonia is of great importance in industrial and agricultural production [1]. Nevertheless, it is an enormous challenge to activate the chemically inert N2 molecules, where a dissociation energy of 945 kJ/mol needs to be overcome [2]. Harsh reaction conditions are employed to directly crack the strong N ≡ N triple bond (high temperature and pressure), causing a large amount of energy consumption and tremendous carbon emission [3]. In contrast, in nature, nitrogen-fixing organisms are able to fix nitrogen to NH3 at ambient temperature and pressure with the existence of nitrogenase (nitrogenase contain MoFe active sites) [4]. Seeking an alternative and sustainable route to promote the activation of NN bond under mild conditions is urgent.

Among various emerging approaches, solar-driven photocatalysis has been used to activate and convert inert molecules via photogenerated electrons and/or holes [5]. Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation (2N2+6H2O = 4NH3+3O2) is carried out through an associative hydrogenation pathway, which is an environmental-friendly and ideal method to synthesize NH3.

Graphitic carbon nitride with sp2 hybridization is a common 2D semiconductor material [6–9]. Compared to g-C3N4, nitrogen-rich g-C3N5 has become one of the hot topics at present due to its special electronic structure and broad optical response [10]. In spite of this, the inherent defects of fast carrier recombination rate and the lack of active sites dramatically hamper its practical application. Researchers have adopted various strategies such as elemental doping, controlling morphology and building heterojunctions [11–13] to overcome these problems. It is desirable to reasonably design catalysts with enhanced activity and superior stability [14]. In this context, carbon dots (CDs) with various functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -CHO) are regarded as promising components in the design of heterojunction catalysts because of their high stability and photoinduced electron transfer properties [15]. Coupling carbon dots with suitable semiconductor substrates can not only facilitate the separation of photogenerated carriers, but also promote nitrogen fixation by introducing metals into carbon dots to provide active centers [16].

Herein, inspired by nitrogenase, we prepared MoFe bimetallic carbon dots loaded g-C3N5, which showed extraordinary photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity (The NH3 yield is up to 812.9 μmol h−1 gcat −1). The introduction of metal carbon dots enhances the efficiency of charge transfer. At the same time, MoFe double active sites provide more possibilities for nitrogen activation and hydrogenation. Synergistic effect of bimetallic carbon dots on nitrogen fixation is demonstrated. On this basis, we propose a feasible catalytic mechanism. This study is expected to provide a valuable reference for constructing high-performance nitrogen fixation catalysts and offer ideas for designing active centers based on the properties of the substrate catalyst.
This section is given in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance and the kinetic profile
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation tests were performed with methanol as a sacrificial agent using a 300 W Xe lamp irradiation at 35 °C for 4 h. As shown in Fig. 1 a, nitrogen-rich g-C3N5 exhibits a superior ammonia formation rate (245.3 μmol h−1 g−1) in comparison with g-C3N4 (74.7 μmol h−1 g−1). The introduction of MoFe bimetallic carbon dots results in the NH3 yield of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 up to 812.9 μmol h−1 gcat −1, which is approximately 3.3 times higher than that of g-C3N5. It is an excellent performance among those reported photocatalysts for photocatalytic nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) (Table S2). After 4 times of cycling test, the NH3 yield rate did not change significantly (Fig. 1b), demonstrating its high stability. More importantly, compared to bimetallic carbon dots, monometallic carbon dots Mo-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (420.6 μmol h−1 gcat −1) and Fe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (554.7 μmol h−1 gcat −1) showed a decrease in nitrogen fixation rate by 48.3 % and 31.8 %, respectively, indicating that the coexistence of MoFe carbon dots favors photocatalytic nitrogen fixation. The synergistic effect of Mo and Fe will be discussed subsequently. An isotopic labelling experiment using 15N2 was carried out to confirm the origin of N in synthesized NH3. In a specific aqueous solution, the generated NH3 can react with phenol forming indophenols, which can be analyzed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [17]. The product obtained with 15N2 as the reactant has a strong mass spectrum peak at m/z = 199, whereas with 14N2, the intensity of the peak at m/z = 198 is stronger (Fig. S1). Additionally, hardly any NH3 was detected with Ar in light condition (Fig. 1c). Therefore, ammonia is indeed produced by photocatalytic nitrogen reduction.

The amount of NH3 produced at different temperature was plotted as a function of the reaction time for MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100, which gives the kinetic profile of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 1c, the NH3 amount increased linearly with time, which suggests that the N2 reduction reaction on MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 can be regarded as a pseudo zero-order reaction. According to the average NH3 generation rate (Fig. 1d) and the total moles of MoFe in the sample (Table S3), the turnover frequency (TOF) at different reaction temperature was obtained (Fig. 1e). As depicted in Fig. 1f, the apparent activation energy (Ea) of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 for NRR is calculated to be 23.4 kJ mol−1 based on Arrhenius equation (refer to the supporting information for details), which is lower than that of the reported NRR catalysts (40–50 kJ mol−1) [18,19], suggesting that the photocatalytic synthesis of ammonia over MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 shows a low kinetic barrier.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were utilized to obtain the texture characteristics of the catalysts. The XRD patterns of g-C3N5 and MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5-X (X = 50, 100, 150) are given in Fig. S2a. Two obvious diffraction peaks at 12.6° and ∼27.7° correspond to the (100) plane (related to the in-plane structure) and the (002) plane (associated with the layer spacing) of g-C3N5 in turn [20]. It is noteworthy that the peak of the (002) plane shifts to a lower angle (from 27.7
∘
° to 27.4°) as the loading of MoFe-CDs increases, while the diffraction peaks of the (100) plane do not move. This implies that the embedding of MoFe-CDs into the interlayers of g-C3N5 leads to a larger layer spacing without affecting the in-plane structural order of g-C3N5. Moreover, no characteristic peaks related to Fe or Mo oxides such as Fe2O3 (PDF#21–0920), FeO (PDF#25–1402) or MoO3 (PDF#09–0209) were observed. It suggests that Fe and Mo species do not get oxidized due to the ligand protection of phenanthroline. We infer that during the solvothermal process, Fe2+ appears to coordinate with N in phenanthroline to form Fe-N bonds, whereas Mo in molybdenum acetylacetonate tends to be retained as Mo-O bonds. From the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2b), it can be seen that the absorption peaks relevant to the vibration of the carbon-nitrogen heterocyclic skeleton (890, 810 cm−1), C = N and CN stretching vibrations (1200–1700 cm−1), and -NH2/-OH stretching (3000–3400 cm−1) were observed in all the cases [20,21]. The results of XRD and FT-IR demonstrate that MoFe-CDs did not change the unit structure as well as functional groups of g-C3N5 (Fig. S2c).

TEM further revealed the location and distribution of MoFe-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a, b), it is obvious to note that MoFe-CDs with a size of ∼30 nm are riveted on abundant mesoporous g-C3N5 nanosheets (Fig. S3) forming a heterojunction. Such structure could facilitate the mass transfer and carrier separation, which is favorable for the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity [22]. The HRTEM image of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (Fig. 2c) shows the lattice fringes of 0.33 nm, assigned to the (002) plane of g-C3N5. Lattice fringes associated with Mo or Fe oxides were not observed. Combining the XRD results, Mo and Fe might form monatomic active centers in the way of bonding to O/N. The elemental mapping of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (Fig. 2d–h) further confirm that Mo and Fe indeed exhibit highly dispersed monatomic distributions, which can fully expose the active sites and improve the catalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric property, band structure and N2 adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The light‐harvesting capability is a factor that affects the nitrogen fixation performance of the catalyst. It is clear to see that g-C3N5 and MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 display superior light absorption compared to g-C3N4, as shown in the ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) (Fig. 3 a). The band gaps of them were obtained from Kubelka−Munk method [23]. From the Tauc plot in Fig. 3b, the band gaps of the three catalysts were measured as 2.40 eV (g-C3N4), 1.90 eV (g-C3N5) and 1.80 eV (MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100). For n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential (EFB) obtained from the Mott-Schottky plot (Fig. 3c) can be regarded as the conduction band potential (ECB) [24,25]. Notably, the conduction band value of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 is more negative, which is beneficial to the occurrence of photocatalytic NRR [26]. By now, the energy band structure of the catalysts is clarified (Fig. 3d). Compared with g-C3N4 and g-C3N5, the photocurrent density (Fig. S4) of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 enhanced significantly, suggesting that the introduction of MoFe-CDs promoted the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. The coupling of g-C3N5 with MoFe-CDs formed a heterogeneous interface, where carriers move directionally and separate. The abundant functional groups and unsaturated bonds on the surface of CDs endow them with a strong electron trapping capacity. Consequently, photogenerated electrons might move towards the metal carbon dots.

The adsorption of N2 on the catalyst includes physisorption and chemisorption, of which chemisorption is recognized as a crucial step in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation [27]. Temperature-programmed desorption of N2 (N2-TPD) was employed to identify the active centers for activating N2 (Fig. 4 a). In the case of g-C3N5, only the physisorption peak of N2 (100 °C) was observed, suggesting that it lacks chemical adsorption sites to activate N2 [28]. Comparatively, Fe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (250, 350 °C), Mo-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (230, 280, 350 °C) and MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (210, 280, 390 °C) exhibited N2 chemisorption peaks in the high-temperature region [29]. This is because the transition metals hold the advantageous ability to bind inert N2 [30]. Obviously, MoFe bimetallic carbon dots can bring about enhanced N2 adsorption as compared to monometallic carbon dots. In addition, the N2 chemisorption of Fe-CDs is superior to that of Mo-CDs [31]. Therefore, we infer that Fe-CDs possess a stronger surface N2 adsorption ability, N2 molecules tends to adsorb to Fe sites (Fig. 5 b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of Mo/Fe sites in carbon dots and reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the role of Mo/Fe sites in carbon dots during the process of NRR, the evolution of the valence and electrochemical environment of the elements before and after the reaction was investigated using XPS. As shown in Fig. S5a, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 can be fitted into four peaks at 284.8 eV (CC), 288.2 eV (N = CN2), 286.1 eV (CNH) and 293.8 eV (π-π*). As for the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S5b), the peaks are attributed to N2C (398.4 eV), N3C (399.6 eV) and C N = N C/CNH2 (400.8 eV), respectively. The binding energies were not shifted after the reaction in the C 1 s and N 1 s spectra, indicating that C/N is not the active centers for the photocatalytic reaction. Considering the precursors 1,10-Phenanthroline and molybdenum acetylacetonate, it may be easier to form Fe-N and Mo-O active centers during high-temperature solvothermal process. Mo and Fe exhibit multivalent states due to unsaturated coordination, which is beneficial to enhance the redox cycle. Molybdenum acetylacetonate might form oxygen vacancies (Mo-OVs), which would contribute to the adsorption and splitting of H2O.

In the XPS spectra of Mo 3d for MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (Fig. 4b), the binding energies of the peaks corresponding to Mo4+ (228.2/234.3 eV) and Mo6+ (231.2/236.8 eV) [32] were hardly changed after the reaction. As to the XPS spectra of Fe 2p3/2 for fresh MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (Fig. 4c), the peaks at 709.4 and 713.0 eV are ascribed to Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively, whereas the peaks at 715.2 and 717.4 eV belong to satellite peaks [33]. For the used MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100, the peaks of Fe2+ (709.4 eV → 708.8 eV) and Fe3+ (713.0 eV → 712.8 eV) were shifted towards low binding energies. There is no doubt that the electron cloud density of Fe increases because of continuous migration by photogenerated electrons from g-C3N5 to Fe sites. It can be seen that in our work, g-C3N5 mainly acts as a photoactive substance to generate photogenerated carriers. Although the metal carbon dots also show a certain optical response, it is less helpful due to the low loading (Table S4). Combined with the above analyses, we suppose that the adsorption, activation and reduction of N2 mainly occur at Fe active centers.

For further definition of the role of Mo-OVs, the XPS spectrum of O 1 s for MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 before and after the reaction were analyzed (Fig. 5a). The two peaks at 532.0 and 533.5 eV are ascribed to O in organic functional groups and -OH in H2O adsorbed on Mo-OVs, respectively [34]. Sacrificial reagent methanol makes it inhibitable for the oxidation of H2O by holes to generate H + [35]. During the reaction, H2O molecules are readily combined with Mo-OVs, thereby facilitating the dissociation of H2O and forming an H +-rich environment. As expected, after the photocatalytic reaction, the peak intensity of -OH increased obviously and this peak shifted towards lower binding energy. Besides, in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier-transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) of g-C3N5, Fe-CDs@g-C3N5–100, Mo-CDs@g-C3N5–100 and MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 (Fig. 6 a–d) provide powerful evidence for the formation of the H +-rich environment. The C–H absorption bands (2950, 2850 cm–1) [36] were detected only in the spectra of the catalysts containing Mo-CDs (Mo-CDs@g-C3N5–100 and MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100), suggesting that Mo-OVs promote the splitting of adsorbed H2O to produce more protons. These protons will migrate and be stored on the neighboring C, forming CH bonds (Fig. 5b). More evidence for the existence of the proton layer is given by Pyridine-thermal desorption-infrared (Py-IR) spectra. All fresh samples (g-C3N5, Fe-CDs@g-C3N5–100, MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100) did not show the characteristic peak of the Bronsted acid site (Fig. S6a). As for the used catalysts, the peak at 1585 cm−1 existing on MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 is assigned to the pyridine adsorbed on Bronsted acid sites (Fig. S6b) [37], which indicated that there are abundant protons on the surface of it. The hydrogenation step is a nonnegligible rate-determining step in the process of N2 fixation to NH3, demonstrating the importance of an efficient protons supply [38,39]. The proton layer on the surface of Mo-CDs can create a local acidic environment around the carbon dots, thereby significantly reducing the energy barrier for the reduction of N2 to NH3, which is in agreement with the kinetic results (The apparent activation energy of the photocatalytic NRR on MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100 is 23.4 kJ mol−1.).

We unraveled the catalytic mechanism via in-situ DRFTIRS of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5–100. Several peaks boosted with the irradiation time increased from 0 to 60 min (Fig. 6d). The absorption bands at 1650 cm−1 corresponds to chemisorbed N2 [40]. σ(NH) and ν(NN) signals can be observed at 1710 and 3480 cm−1 in turn [41]. Characteristic absorption of NH4 + was detected at 1340 cm−1 [42], which is powerful evidence for the generation of ammonia. The peaks assigned to H2NNH2 (1060, 1120 cm−1) [36] confirm that the catalyst takes the associative alternating pathway for N2 reduction [43,44].

Based on the above discussion, a feasible catalytic mechanism is proposed. During the photocatalytic reaction, N2 will undergo strong chemisorption on Fe sites of MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5, causing the polarization of the NN triple bond (Eq (1)). Upon light irradiation, electron-hole pairs are generated on the surface of g-C3N5 (Eq (2)). Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons in the excited state transfer rapidly to Fe-CDs (Eq (3)), whereas holes will be captured by the sacrificial agent (methanol). N2 adsorbed on Fe sites are further activated by the attack of photogenerated electrons (Eq (4)). Additionally, Mo-OVs will split adsorbed H2O and there is a proton layer formed on Mo-CDs. With the provision of protons, the activated N2 is gradually reduced to ammonia by the associative alternating pathway (Eqs. (5), (6)). Fig. 7 illustrates the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation process on MoFe-CDs@g-C3N5.
(1) MoFe-CDs + N2 → N2-MoFe-CDs
(2) g-C3N5 + hν → g-C3N5 (e −) + g-C3N5 (h +)
(3) g-C3N5 (e −) + N2-MoFe-CDs → g-C3N5 + N2-MoFe-CDs (e −)
(4) N2-MoFe-CDs (e −) → *N2-MoFe-CDs
(5) *N2-MoFe-CDs + 2e−+ 2H+→ *HNNH-MoFe-CDs
(6) *HNNH-MoFe-CDs + 6e−+ 6H+→ 2NH4 ++ MoFe-CDs (e −)

In this work, we loaded MoFe bimetallic carbon dots onto g-C3N5 to form heterojunction catalysts with excellent photocatalytic N2 fixation performance. The photogenerated electrons can be rapidly transferred to Fe active sites of the carbon dots across the heterojunction interface, which improves the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers. Through further studies, it was found that MoFe bimetallic carbon dots play synergistic roles, in which Fe sites are mainly responsible for the adsorption and activation of N2, while Mo sites tend to split H2O to produce protons. The proton layer on Mo-CDs makes the local environment to be more acidic, thus reducing the activation energy of nitrogen reduction to ammonia. Eventually, a possible catalytic mechanism was proposed depending on the detection of the intermediates. This study offers guidance for the design of nitrogen fixation catalysts with novel carbon dots types of active centers.

Jianuan Wen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kang Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuchao Shan: Formal analysis. Wei Cai: Formal analysis. Hongxia Qu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51778296) and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_0501).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mcat.2024.114165.

Chin. J. Catal.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Constructing electron transfer pathways and active centers over W18O49 nanowires by doping Fe3+ and incorporating g-C3N5 for enhanced photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
Inorg. Chem. Front.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, M. Li, Q. Zhang, F. Li, L.
